elements.sent-2411-001: Time-reversibility is a crucial feature of many time series models, while time-irreversibility is the rule rather than the exception in real-life data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Testing the null hypothesis of time-reversibilty, therefore, should be an important step preliminary to the identification and estimation of most traditional time-series models.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing procedures, however, mostly consist of testing necessary but not sufficient conditions, leading to under-rejection, or sufficient but non-necessary ones, which leads to over-rejection.
elements.sent-2411-005: In contrast, the copula spectrum studied by Goto et al.~(Ann.. Statist.
elements.sent-2411-006: A test based on this copula-spectrum-based characterization has been proposed by authors.
elements.sent-2411-007: This paper illustrates the performance of this test, with an illustration in the analysis of climatic data.
elements.sent-2411-008: Time-reversibility (sometimes referred to as {\it lag-reversibility or {\it non-directionality : see, e.g., \cite{Lawrance91 ) is a crucial feature of most classical time-series models---a feature which, however, is challenged by many real-life processes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Examples include observations from various fields: unemployment rates and the business cycle \citep{neftci84 , electroencephalographic and heartbeat recordings \citep{VderH96, Palus96, Costaetal05 , epidemic outbreaks \citep{Grenfelletal94 , ...}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: This observation has prompted researchers to investigate time-reversibility more closely.
elements.sent-2411-011: Several approaches have been developed to test the null hypothesis of time-reversibility.
elements.sent-2411-012: The converse is true, provided that the distribution of the process is determined by its moments.
elements.sent-2411-013: Tests based on bi- and tri-spectra are considered by \cite{hr98 and \cite{wfh2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: We refer to \cite{Kathpaliaetal21 or Chapter 8 of \cite{goojier2017 for a comprehensive review of the problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Many of these tests are based on necessary but not sufficient conditions for time-reversibility, which unavoidably induces blind spots, hence biasedness and under-rejection.
elements.sent-2411-017: Tests based on necessary and sufficient conditions, thus, are highly desirable.
elements.sent-2411-018: This is what the copula spectrum develo\-ped by \cite{gkvvdh22 , which offers a model-free characterization of pairwise time-reversibility, is allowing for.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this paper, we illustrate the performance of the copula-spectrum-based tests proposed by \cite{gkvvdh22 in comparison with the recent model-based approach by \cite{ghm22 , who are using it to assess the time-reversibility of climate-related time-series data.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The paper is organized as follows: Section \ref{sec::TR reviews the approaches developed by \cite{gkvvdh22 and by \cite{ghm22 , respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Section \ref{sec::NS presents our numerical results, and Section \ref{sec::ES compares the conclusions of the two approaches on the climate-related application considered in \cite{ghm22 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A strictly stationary process $\proc$ is said to be {\it pairwise time-reversible if the distribution of $(X_t,X_{t+k )$ is equal to that of $(X_{t+k ,X_t)$ for all~$k\in\mathbb Z$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: We are interested in the pairwise time-reversibility of the process and consider testing \begin{equation}H_0: \proc is pairwise time-reversible\end{equation} against \begin{equation}H_1:\ \proc is not pairwise time-reversible .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the sequel, for the sake of simplicity, we write {\it time-reversibility and {\it time-reversible for {\it pairwise time-reversibility and {\it pairwise time-reversible .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: A process $\proc$ which is not pairwise time-reversible will be referred to as {\it pairwise time-irreversible ---in short, {\it time-irreversible or {\it non-reversible .}}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: A test based on the integrated copula spectrum
elements.sent-2411-026: A test for time-reversibility based on the {\it integrated copula spectrum (hereafter referred to as~ICS) with critical values obtained via a subsampling method was proposed by \cite{gkvvdh22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The ICS is defined, for a strictly stationary process $\proc$ with marginal distribution function $F$, as \begin{equation}\specdis(\lambda ;\tau_1,\tau_2)\coloneqq \int_0^{\lambda}\spec(\omega; \tau_1,\tau_2)\dd\omega , \qquad(\tau_1,\tau_2)\in (0,1)^2,\ \ \lambda\in [0,\pi],\end{equation} where $\mathfrak{f}$ is the {\it copula spectral density , given by ($I\{A\}$ stands for the indicator of $A$) \begin{equation}f(\omega; \tau_1,\tau_2)\coloneqq \frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k \in \Z}{\rm Cov}(I\{F(X_k) \leq \tau_1\}, I\{F(X_0) \leq \tau_2\})e^{-i\omega k},\quad (\omega,\tau_1,\tau_2)\in \R\times(0,1)^2\!.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-028: As an estimator of $\specdis$, we propose \begin{equation}\widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\!}_{n,R}(\lambda ;\tau_1,\tau_2)\coloneqq {} \frac{2\pi}{n}\sum_{s=1}^{n-1}I\big\{0\leq \frac{2\pi s}{n}\leq\lambda\big\}\copper_{n,R}^{\tau_1,\tau_2}\big(\frac{2\pi s}{n}\big),\qquad\lambda\in [0,\pi],\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\copper_{n,R}^{\tau_1,\tau_2}(\omega)\coloneqq \frac{1}{2\pi n}d_{n,R}^{\tau_1}(\omega)d_{n,R}^{\tau_2}(-\omega),\qquad (\omega,\tau_1,\tau_2)\in \R\times(0,1)^2\end{equation} with \begin{equation}d_{n,R}^{\tau}(\omega)\coloneqq \sum_{t=0}^{n-1}I\{\hat{F}_n(X_t)\leq\tau\}e^{-i\omega t} and  \hat{F}_n(x)\coloneqq \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=0}^{n-1}I\{X_t\leq x\}\end{equation} is the {\it copula rank periodogram .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our test statistic accordingly is defined as \begin{equation}T_{\rm TR}^{(n)}\coloneqq \sqrt{n} \max_{(\lambda, \tau_1, \tau_2) \in \mathcal T}\Big| {\Im \widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\!}_{n,R}(\lambda, \tau_1, \tau_2)}\Big|,\end{equation} where $\Im \widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\! _{n,R}$ denotes the imaginary part of $\widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\! _{n,R}$ and \begin{equation}\mathcal T\coloneqq \{2\pi \ell/32; \ell = 0,1,\ldots,16\}\times\{j/32; j=1,\ldots,31\}^2\end{equation} is a discrete grid {(16337 gridpoints) over~$[0,\pi]\times(0,1)^2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The p-value $p_{{\rm TR }$ is calculated via the subsampling procedure: \begin{equation}\label{p-val} p_{{\rm TR}}\coloneqq \frac{1}{n-b+1} \sum_{t=0}^{n-b}I\big\{T_{{\rm TR}}^{(n,b,t)}> T_{\rm TR}^{(n)}\big\},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}T_{\rm TR}^{(n,b,t)}\coloneqq \left(1 - \frac{b}{n}\right)^{-1/2}\sqrt{b}\max_{ (\lambda, \tau_1, \tau_2) \in \mathcal T}\Big| {\Im \widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\!}_{n,b,t,R}(\lambda, \tau_1, \tau_2)}\Big|, \widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\!}_{n,b,t,R}(\lambda ;\tau_1,\tau_2) \coloneqq \frac{2\pi}{b} \sum_{j=1}^{b-1} I\big\{0\leq \frac{2\pi j}{b}\leq\lambda\big\} \copper_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_1,\tau_2}\Big( \frac{2\pi j}{b} \Big), \copper_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_1,\tau_2}(\omega)\coloneqq \frac{1}{2\pi b}d_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_1}(\omega)d_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_2}(-\omega),\qquad (\omega,\tau_1,\tau_2)\in \R\times(0,1)^2, d_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau}(\omega)\coloneqq \sum_{j=0}^{b-1}I\{\hat{F}_{n,b,t}(X_{t+j})\leq\tau\}e^{-i\omega j},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\widehat F_{n,b,t}(x) \coloneqq \frac{1}{b} \sum_{i=t}^{t+b-1} I\{X_{i} \leq x\};\end{equation} the block length $b$ is determined by the rule of thumb \begin{equation}\label{eqn:rt_bw} b^{\rm rt}_n \coloneqq \max\{ 2^j : 2^j \leq 2n^{2/3}, \ j=4,\ldots,8 \}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}T_{\rm TR}^{(n,b,t)}\coloneqq \left(1 - \frac{b}{n}\right)^{-1/2}\sqrt{b}\max_{ (\lambda, \tau_1, \tau_2) \in \mathcal T}\Big| {\Im \widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\!}_{n,b,t,R}(\lambda, \tau_1, \tau_2)}\Big|, \widehat{\specdis}\phantom{F\!\!\!\!\!}_{n,b,t,R}(\lambda ;\tau_1,\tau_2) \coloneqq \frac{2\pi}{b} \sum_{j=1}^{b-1} I\big\{0\leq \frac{2\pi j}{b}\leq\lambda\big\} \copper_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_1,\tau_2}\Big( \frac{2\pi j}{b} \Big), \copper_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_1,\tau_2}(\omega)\coloneqq \frac{1}{2\pi b}d_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_1}(\omega)d_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau_2}(-\omega),\qquad (\omega,\tau_1,\tau_2)\in \R\times(0,1)^2, d_{n,b,t,R}^{\tau}(\omega)\coloneqq \sum_{j=0}^{b-1}I\{\hat{F}_{n,b,t}(X_{t+j})\leq\tau\}e^{-i\omega j},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-031: The test rejects $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha$ whenever $p_{{\rm TR <\alpha$.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Under mild regularity conditions, ICS-based tests have asymptotically correct size and are consistent against any fixed alternative in $H_1$: see Theorem 4.2 in \cite{gkvvdh22 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Their test involves the following steps.
elements.sent-2411-034: Step 1 Fit a causal AR($p$) model to the data (order $p$ determined by BIC).
elements.sent-2411-035: Step 2 Apply the Shapiro-Wilk or/and the Jarque-Bera test \citep{ShapWilk65,JBera80 of normality to the residuals of the fitted AR($p$) model.}
elements.sent-2411-036: If the hypothesis of normality is not rejected, conclude that the data is time-reversible; otherwise, proceed to Step~3.
elements.sent-2411-037: Step 3 Considering an AR($r+s$) model with unspecified mean $\mu$, $r$ causal roots, and~$s$ non-causal ones, driven by scaled i.i.d.~Student noise\footnote{The driving noise is of the form $\sigma\varepsilon_t$, where $\sigma >0$ is an unspecified scale and $\varepsilon_t$ is i.i.d.\ Student with unspecified degrees of freedom $\nu$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: If the estimators of $r$ and $s$ differ,\footnote{Since $r$ and $s$ and their estimators $\hat r$ and $\hat s$ are integers, consistency implies that, for $n$ large enough, $\hat{r =r$ and $\hat{s =s$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Step 4 Based on a further constrained Student likelihood with the additional constraint that~$r=s$ and the $r=s$ causal and non-causal AR parameters are equal, re-estimate~$\mu$,~$\nu$,~$\sigma^2$, and the unknown AR parameters and proceed to one of the following steps.
elements.sent-2411-040: Step 5 (Strategy 1) If the BIC for the unrestricted likelihood in Step~3 is strictly smaller than that for the restricted likelihood in Step~4, conclude that the series is time-irreversible; otherwise, conclude that it is time-reversible.
elements.sent-2411-041: Step 5 (Strategy 2) Based on the Student likelihoods computed in Steps~3 and~4, perform a likelihood ratio test for the hypothesis that the causal and non-causal parameters are equal.
elements.sent-2411-042: If the hypothesis is rejected, conclude that the data is time-irreversible; otherwise, conclude that it is time-reversible.
elements.sent-2411-043: This procedure is it entirely heuristic---no consistency results are proven in \cite{ghm22 ---and its global size, when all steps are performed at nominal size $\alpha$, is anything but clear; however, it seems to work well, at least when the data-generating process is linear.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This section presents the respective simulation-based finite-sample performance of the ICS and GHM methods, with a comparative discussion.
elements.sent-2411-045: In our simulations, we considered both positively and negatively correlated Beta random variables autoregressive processes (PBAR and NBAR, see \cite{McK85 ) of the form \begin{equation}\label{BAR} X_t = 1-U_t(1-W_t X_{t-1})\quadand\quad X_t = U_t(1-W_t X_{t-1}),\quad t\in\Z ,\end{equation} where $\{U_t\}$ and $\{W_t\}$, $t\in\Z$ are i.i.d.\ ${\rm Beta (2,1)$ and ${\rm Beta (1,1)$ random variables, respectively, and $\{U_t\}$ is independent of $\{W_t\}$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{BAR} X_t = 1-U_t(1-W_t X_{t-1})\quadand\quad X_t = U_t(1-W_t X_{t-1}),\quad t\in\Z ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: The PBAR and NBAR models, proposed by \cite{McK85 , are non-linear and non-Gaussian but time-reversible, satisfying $H_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: So, it is expected that the ICS and GHM procedures do not reject $H_0$.
elements.sent-2411-048: We also considered the quantile autoregressive (QAR) model proposed by \cite{Koenker2006 , which is an example of nonlinear, uncorrelated, and time-irreversible model, under which~$H_0$ should be rejected.}
elements.sent-2411-049: More precisely, in our simulations, we considered the~QAR(1) model \begin{equation}\label{QAR} X_t=0.1\Phi^{-1}(U_t)+1.9(U_t-0.5)X_{t-1},\end{equation} where $\{U_t\}$ is i.i.d.\ uniform over $[0,1]$ and $\Phi $, as usual, stands for the the standard normal distribution function.
elements.sent-2411-050: In the first step of the GHM procedure, BIC is computed for $p=0,1,\ldots,5$.
elements.sent-2411-051: When applied to simulated QAR(1) series, BIC with high probability is selecting $p=0$---that is, an i.i.d.\ AR(0) process---in which case the GHM procedure logically should conclude to time-reversibility (although nothing is mentioned about this in \cite{ghm22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: The subsampling block length for the ICS procedure is determined by \eqref{eqn:rt_bw .}
elements.sent-2411-053: We applied the ICS and GHM (strategies 1 and~2) methods to data generated from the three models \eqref{BAR and~\eqref{QAR with series lengths~$n=100, 200, 500$, and~$1000$, each with 1000 replications.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: All tests were performed at nominal level $\alpha = 5\%$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Rejection frequencies are reported in Table~\ref{fig:plot1 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The left and center panels are for the PBAR and NBAR models \eqref{BAR , respectively, which are time-reversible.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Rejection frequencies, thus, should be less than the nominal size $5\%$.
elements.sent-2411-058: While the empirical size of the ICS method, irrespective of $n$, satisfies this level constraint, both GHM strategies grossly violate it.
elements.sent-2411-059: The right panel is dealing with the QAR model \eqref{QAR , the time-irreversibility of which is very well detected, for all $n$, by the ICS method while the two GHM strategies yield extremely low powers.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Whether they reject time-reversibility or not, the conclusions of the GHM tests, thus, should be taken with some care.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width = \linewidth, height = 0.34 \linewidth]{res_pval.pdf} \end{center} \caption{ Rejection frequencies (vertical axis) of the integrated copula-spectrum-based test (ICS, black solid line \protect\solidcircle) and the model-based tests (GHM strategy 1, red dashed line \protect\dashedtriangle and GHM strategy~2, green dotted line \protect\dottedplus) in 1000 replications of the PBAR (left panel; $H_0$ satisfied), NBAR (center panel; $H_0$ satisfied), and QAR (right panel; $H_0$ violated) processes with series lengths~$n=100, 200, 500$, and~$1000$ (horizontal axis). All tests are performed at nominal level $\alpha = 5\%$.} \label{fig:plot1}
elements.sent-2411-061: We investigate the time-reversibility of climate change-related data previously analyzed in \cite{ghm22 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Table \ref{tab:1 provides a summary of the data, including abbreviations, observation periods, and observation frequencies (yearly or monthly).}
elements.sent-2411-063: For GLO, GL, GO, SA, GHG, N2O, GCAG, GISTEMP, and GMSL, as in \cite{ghm22 , we apply the Hodrick--Prescott filter \citep{hp97 , with smoothing parameter $\lambda=14400$ for monthly data and $\lambda=100$ for yearly data, to remove trends.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The remaining series (SOI, NAO, PDO, NH, SH) do not exhibit any apparent trend.
elements.sent-2411-065: Table~\ref{tab:2 summarizes the sample sizes and the subsampling block sizes selected by \eqref{eqn:rt_bw .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We apply the methods introduced in Section \ref{sec::TR to the detrended data (GLO, GL, GO, SA, GHG, N2O, GCAG, GISTEMP, GMSL) and the raw data (SOI, NAO, PDO, NH, SH).}
elements.sent-2411-067: The $p$-values obtained for the ICS method are reported in Table \ref{tab:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: There is strong evidence of time-irreversibility for the GO (global ocean temperature anomaly) and SH (southern hemisphere sea ice area) series.
elements.sent-2411-069: In contrast, the GHM method concludes that the GO and SH series, but also the GLO, GL, SA, and NAO ones are time-reversible, whereas time-irreversibility is detected in GHG, N2O, GCAG, GISTEMP, GMSL, SOI, PDO, and NH.
elements.sent-2411-070: No $p$-value is associated with these conclusions, though.
elements.sent-2411-071: The detail is below: GLO, GL, GO, SA, and SH are declared time-reversible because Gaussianity could not be rejected in Step~2, neither by the Shapiro-Wilk test nor by the Jarque-Bera one.
elements.sent-2411-072: Non-rejection of Gaussianity, however, does not imply Gaussianity, and these series still could be non-Gaussian and time-irreversible.
elements.sent-2411-073: In view of the ICS results, GO and SH may indeed fall into this case.
elements.sent-2411-074: For all other series, Gaussianity is rejected in Step~2, both by the Shapiro-Wilk and the Jarque-Bera test.
elements.sent-2411-075: For GHG, GCAG, GISTEMP, N2O, GMSL, and PDO, the causal and non-causal AR($r+s$) model orders selected by Student maximum likelihood in Step~3 do not satisfy $r=s$, hence the GHM method concludes to the time-irreversibility of these series.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, the possibility remains that these processes are non-linear yet time-reversible.
elements.sent-2411-077: For SOI and NAO, the orders $r$ and $s$ selected by Student maximum likelihood in Step~3 are the same.
elements.sent-2411-078: While strategies 1 and 2 both conclude that SOI is time-irreversible, there remains a possibility it is non-linear but time-reversible.
elements.sent-2411-079: Interestingly, BIC for the restricted likelihood for NAO is smaller than BIC for the unrestricted one, and the equality of causal and non-causal parameters is not rejected by the likelihood ratio test in strategy 2, yielding the conclusion that NAO is time-reversible.
elements.sent-2411-080: The two methods, thus, yield quite different conclusions; while the reliability of the ICS conclusions is controlled by a type one risk less than $5\%$, though, no risk evaluation is possible for the GHM method.
